header.title: Daily FI Market Intelligence
header.stance: Trump's Hormuz blockade and Waller's tightening signal lifted oil, real yields and the policy path.

overall_summary.headline: A physical oil shock and a named Fed signal tightened the policy horizon.
overall_summary.summary: Trump reinstated the blockade of Iranian ships transiting Hormuz and demanded a 20% charge on other cargo, while Waller said another hot core-inflation print could require near-term tightening. Oil rose about 11%, the 5Y led Treasury losses, and both real yields and breakevens increased as semiconductors, listed credit and EM debt weakened.
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: Trump's reinstated Iran blockade and proposed 20% Hormuz cargo charge widened the physical energy premium. Waller's near-term tightening signal carried that shock into Fed pricing, the Treasury belly and real yields, while technology, listed credit and EM debt marked the broader increase in capital costs.

watch_events.title: CPI And Warsh Testimony
watch_events.watch_line: Core inflation and the Fed reaction function set the persistence of Waller's tightening signal.
watch_events.market_trigger: Core CPI composition and Warsh's treatment of energy pass-through, labor and inflation expectations.
watch_events.market_implication: Firm core CPI extends belly pressure; softer core inflation shifts the move toward energy.
watch_events.confirms_if: Firm core CPI, restrictive testimony and higher 2Y, 5Y and real yields.
watch_events.challenges_if: Softer core CPI and lower real yields despite elevated oil.
watch_events.why_it_matters: This is the nearest policy test of the July 13 close.

watch_events.title: PPI And Beige Book
watch_events.watch_line: Pipeline prices and regional pricing power follow the CPI composition test.
watch_events.market_trigger: Core PPI, energy-sensitive inputs and firms' ability to pass costs through.
watch_events.market_implication: Firm costs preserve restrictive real rates and credit concessions; weaker pricing power supports duration.
watch_events.confirms_if: Firm producer prices and persistent business pricing power.
watch_events.challenges_if: Easing input costs with weaker regional demand.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: UST belly, real yields and investment-grade credit
watch_events.why_it_matters: The pair measures whether the oil impulse is entering a broader cost pipeline.

watch_events.title: Retail Sales And Claims
watch_events.watch_line: Demand and labor show how much restrictive real rates the economy can absorb.
watch_events.market_trigger: Retail momentum and the direction of initial claims.
watch_events.market_implication: Resilient activity supports policy restraint; weaker consumption and rising claims cap real yields and challenge credit.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Front-end Treasuries, high yield and cyclicals
watch_events.why_it_matters: The releases separate inflation tightening from a broader growth slowdown.

watch_events.title: Treasury Bill Liquidity
watch_events.watch_line: Bill auctions measure cash demand through the inflation and policy event window.
watch_events.market_trigger: Stop levels, demand and concessions in 17-week, 4-week, 8-week, 3-month and 6-month supply.
watch_events.market_implication: Deep demand contains front-end event premium; weak clearing extends the repricing through cash maturities.
watch_events.challenges_if: Higher clearing yields and weaker demand across maturities.
watch_events.why_it_matters: Cash allocation determines whether the policy event remains orderly.

watch_events.title: BOK And Friday Macro Checks
watch_events.watch_line: Asian policy, housing, production and inflation expectations complete the global sequence.
watch_events.market_trigger: BOK guidance, housing starts, industrial production and Michigan inflation expectations.
watch_events.market_implication: The releases map imported energy pressure, rate-sensitive demand and household inflation credibility.
watch_events.confirms_if: Contained expectations and stable activity under credible policy.
watch_events.challenges_if: Currency pressure or higher expectations require tighter policy.
watch_events.sensitive_channel: Asian front ends, UST duration and breakevens
watch_events.why_it_matters: The sequence shows how far the U.S. shock reaches into global policy.

cross_asset_cards.title: Oil
cross_asset_cards.summary: Higher crude and firmer breakevens pushed against duration.

cross_asset_cards.title: Credit / EM
cross_asset_cards.summary: Listed LQD/HYG/JNK/EMB rows fell with rate drag. Spreads, books, and borrower access carry the credit read.

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold
cross_asset_cards.summary: Treasury yields were higher, and dollar firmness tightened financial conditions.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening Fixed-Income Thesis

Two named catalysts organized the July 13 close. President Donald Trump reinstated the U.S. blockade of Iranian ships transiting the Strait of Hormuz and demanded a 20% reimbursement on other cargo, adding a direct shipping-cost proposal to renewed U.S.-Iran strikes and conflicting claims over access to the strait. Fed Governor Christopher Waller then put the policy consequence in explicit terms: another hot core-inflation reading could require near-term tightening. WTI and Brent each rose about 11%, the 5-year Treasury yield led a broad selloff, and money markets moved toward roughly even odds of a July rate increase. The session repriced both the physical cost of energy and the Federal Reserve's response to persistent inflation.

The Treasury distribution reinforced that reading. The 5-year led with a 7-basis-point rise, while real yields increased more than breakevens and the long bond lagged the belly. Inflation compensation widened, but tighter policy and a higher real discount rate did more work than energy expectations alone. Firmer three- to twelve-month bill yields placed the same pressure inside the prospective policy horizon.

The shock spread beyond rates. Semiconductors led the equity decline, volatility rose, DXY strengthened, and gold, listed credit and EM debt closed lower. The market charged long-duration growth, Treasury-duration credit and external-dollar borrowers at the same time. AI demand remained strong, yet SK Hynix's post-listing selloff and the scale of new data-center commitments exposed the absorption problem behind the next financing wave.

CPI and Kevin Warsh's congressional testimony now sit directly on top of the repricing. Core inflation determines whether the tightening signal extends beyond the oil impulse; headline composition determines how much of the move remains in breakevens; Warsh's description of the reaction function determines how much event premium stays in real yields and the belly. PPI, the Beige Book, retail sales, claims and bill auctions follow immediately. The close left a more expensive policy path, a physical energy premium with uncertain implementation and less room for capital-intensive borrowers to rely on benign rates.

## UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure

The nominal curve cheapened across every benchmark, with the largest losses in intermediate maturities. The 2-year yield closed at 4.26%, up 5 basis points; the 5-year at 4.37%, up 7 basis points; the 10-year at 4.62%, up 6 basis points; and the 30-year at 5.10%, up 4 basis points. The 2s10s slope widened 1 basis point to 36 basis points, while 2s30s narrowed 1 basis point to 84 basis points. Those modest slope changes concealed the more useful distribution: the 5-year sector carried the greatest outright adjustment, and the long bond remained above 5% without leading the selloff.

Waller supplied the most concrete policy catalyst. He said another hot core-inflation reading could require the FOMC to consider near-term tightening. Money markets moved to about 50% odds of a July increase, while separate local reporting described a September increase as almost fully priced, compared with roughly two-thirds probability one week earlier. Those probabilities are contextual rather than checked market rows, but their direction matches the Treasury board. The Fed debate moved from patience around declining inflation toward the timing of renewed restraint.

The timing of the remark sharpened its effect. Markets received the tightening signal one day before CPI and before Warsh's first congressional testimony as chair, so the curve had to carry both the probability of a stronger inflation print and uncertainty about the Fed's response. The statement did not announce a fixed path; it supplied a threshold tied to broad core pressure. That distinction left the 2-year exposed to the next decision while concentrating more cumulative policy risk in the 5-year.

Curvature put the same adjustment in sharper form. The 2s5s10s butterfly rose 3 basis points to minus 14 basis points, and the 2s10s30s butterfly rose 3 basis points to minus 12 basis points. The belly cheapened against both wings because the combination of the Fed signal, resilient activity and the oil shock changed the expected policy path over several years. The 30-year still retained substantial term and fiscal compensation at 5.10%, but the incremental pressure landed where a renewed tightening phase has the greatest cumulative effect.

Real yields and breakevens both contributed. The 10-year TIPS yield rose 4 basis points to 2.36%; the 10-year breakeven rose 2 basis points to 2.26%. Reasoning-only Treasury rows showed 5- and 7-year real yields also up 4 basis points, with 20- and 30-year real yields up 3 basis points. Energy restored inflation premium across the curve, while a stronger anticipated policy response lifted the real rate applied to investment and duration. The session therefore tightened financial conditions through two channels at once: a higher expected price path and a higher real discount rate.

Bill maturities confirmed that the adjustment was not concentrated in overnight cash. Four- and six-week coupon-equivalent yields rose only 1 basis point to 3.70%, and the 8-week yield was unchanged at 3.72%. The 13-week yield rose 5 basis points to 3.85%, the 26-week rose 4 basis points to 4.00%, and the 52-week rose 6 basis points to 4.12%. Cash closest to the current setting stayed comparatively anchored; maturities extending through the prospective tightening window demanded more compensation.

Warsh's testimony on July 14 and 15 follows that signal with a communication test. Warsh has resisted conventional forward guidance, while investors have pressed for a clearer account of the Fed's reaction function across inflation, labor and growth. CPI arrives before the first appearance, so the chairman will speak with a fresh inflation composition already in the market. Firm core CPI extends belly and real-yield pressure; softer core inflation supports relief even with headline energy risk. The response will establish whether the July 13 move becomes a durable policy repricing or remains concentrated around the energy event.

## Credit, Funding, And Primary-Market Absorption

Listed credit weakened, but the more informative credit signal came from the interaction between AI equity, capital intensity and financing capacity. LQD fell 0.47%, HYG lost 0.24%, and JNK declined 0.18%. These rows mark listed-market tone rather than cash-spread proof. The larger move in semiconductors, with the SOX down 4.78%, showed where higher real yields and weaker risk tolerance were most concentrated.

SK Hynix captured the tension. The company raised $26.5 billion in the largest U.S. listing by a foreign issuer, yet its American depositary receipts fell 9.3% in U.S. hours after Seoul shares dropped as much as 15%. The equity repricing did not reflect a collapse in end demand: TSMC reported a 36% increase in quarterly sales to NT$1.27 trillion. Investors instead charged more for valuation, capital intensity and the funding required to sustain the buildout. Strong operating demand and weaker market absorption coexisted.

That distinction extends to debt. Hyperscalers have financed much of their infrastructure through large unsecured investment-grade bond sales, but source reporting described investors approaching practical absorption limits. Data-center bonds, tenant-backed structures, private credit and other channels are becoming more important as the required capital expands. Meta added another $40 billion to its Louisiana data-center commitment, taking expected site investment above $250 billion. Scale remains available, but it increasingly requires collateral, contracted cash flow or a wider investor base rather than repeated reliance on unsecured corporate balance sheets.

The change in structure also changes the underwriting question. An unsecured hyperscaler bond rests on the issuer's consolidated balance sheet; a data-center bond shifts attention toward the tenant's ability to pay, contract durability, asset completion and residual value. That can broaden capacity without making capital cheap. Higher Treasury yields raise the base rate for every channel, while weaker technology equity reduces the market value supporting the most aggressive expansion plans.

Pinewood offers a smaller test of market breadth. The U.K. studio met investors about a possible fixed-rate sterling bond, subject to market conditions. Its existing £750 million note matures in 2030 and carries a 6% coupon; ratings split between BB- at S&P and BBB- at Fitch. Order-book depth, final spread, tenor and secondary trading will show how far primary access extends beyond sovereigns and the largest technology issuers after the global rates selloff. Clean execution preserves a broader financing window; a larger concession narrows it toward borrowers with the strongest structure and sponsorship.

## Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission

Japan removed one potential source of immediate domestic-duration demand. The yen weakened as much as 0.4% to 162.36 per dollar and Japanese government bonds extended losses after a report that Japan had no plan to change the Government Pension Investment Fund's allocation. Expectations of a home-bias shift had grown after Finance Minister Satsuki Katayama encouraged large pension funds to invest more domestically. GPIF, with roughly $1.8 trillion of assets, still maintains the allocation adopted in 2025: one quarter each in domestic bonds, domestic equities, foreign bonds and foreign equities.

The clarification matters through global savings allocation, not through a mechanical forecast of Treasury flows. An immediate rotation out of foreign duration and into JGBs is no longer the base assumption. The yen and JGB response showed that investors had attached real value to that prospect. Japan's long-end fiscal and normalization debate remains active, but the July 13 information removed a near-term institutional allocation catalyst.

Europe faced a different duration constraint. Economists cut their 2026 euro-area growth estimate to 0.5% from 0.7%, below the ECB's 0.8% baseline, after renewed Hormuz disruption weakened the expected energy recovery. Their median inflation forecast remained 2.8%, well above the ECB's 2% target. Yannis Stournaras described the renewed fighting as taking the central bank back to square one. Slower growth supports duration through demand; imported energy inflation restricts the easing response and preserves compensation in nominal yields.

That mix also affects fiscal transmission. More expensive imported energy reduces real household income and raises public pressure for subsidies or tax relief just as slower growth weakens revenue. The resulting duration supply does not need to match the U.S. move day for day to matter. It raises the compensation European curves require from governments and limits the amount of global duration relief available when U.S. yields rise.

The combined global picture remained fragmented. Japan lacked an immediate home-bias shift, Europe faced a weaker growth and higher energy-cost mix, and the U.S. real-yield shock raised the global discount rate. None of those channels supplied a broad offset to the Treasury selloff. Country allocation, fiscal credibility and central-bank reaction functions continued to set developed-market duration separately.

## EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk

The common EM backdrop tightened as DXY rose 0.31% and EMB fell 0.64%, but country access remained differentiated. Higher oil raised the import bill for energy consumers, higher U.S. real yields increased the hurdle for dollar debt, and a firmer dollar reduced room for domestic easing. The selected sovereign stories still showed capital available where policy anchors and debt management were credible.

Indonesia retained its investment-grade rating and stable outlook at S&P. The agency cited fiscal discipline across administrations and expected the 3% budget-deficit ceiling to remain an important anchor. That affirmation arrived after a June bond selloff tied to interventionist policy and spending concerns. Indonesian bonds had delivered a 10% hedged dollar loss for the year, so the rating decision did not erase market damage; it preserved the institutional constraint supporting the credit profile.

Chile entered the window from a stronger market position. Its dollar spread had tightened 24 basis points since the end of March to about 82 basis points, near levels last seen in 2007, while default-protection costs fell below their level before the U.S. airstrikes on Iran. The Senate authorized an additional $6.2 billion of borrowing, much of it expected abroad. Tight spreads and foreign demand provided room to issue despite Middle East escalation and weak domestic growth.

Nigeria and Ethiopia responded through policy restraint. Standard Chartered raised its forecast for average Nigerian inflation in 2026 to 15.5% from 12% and expected only 150 basis points of easing, leaving the policy rate at 25% by year-end from 26.5% currently. Ethiopia raised its benchmark to 16% from 15% and removed a 24% annual credit-growth ceiling while retaining a tight stance aimed at single-digit inflation. One central bank slowed the expected easing cycle; the other tightened outright while changing the credit-control framework. Both placed credibility ahead of near-term funding relief.

Nigeria's inflation-perception index added a domestic expectations signal, rising to 45 in June from 44.8 in May. Ethiopia's removal of the credit-growth ceiling did not offset the rate increase; it changed the instrument mix while keeping aggregate policy restrictive. These details distinguish formal policy rates from the broader transmission into bank lending, local-currency demand and sovereign funding costs.

Kenya used liability management. Officials considered buying back as much as $500 million of eurobonds and financing the purchase with new dollar debt to extend maturities. The transaction would be Kenya's fourth buyback in two years. The structure reduces near-term repayment concentration only when the new issue clears at sustainable terms and the maturity extension offsets the funding cost. Higher U.S. yields and oil make pricing, order-book breadth and post-deal performance central to the result.

The section therefore remained a country-selection market rather than a broad liquidity trade. Indonesia and Chile showed the value of fiscal anchors and established demand; Nigeria and Ethiopia showed the price of inflation credibility; Kenya showed active maturity management. DXY and EMB set a less forgiving common backdrop, leaving domestic execution to determine who keeps access.

## Cross asset

Trump's Hormuz announcement gave the oil shock a specific policy form. The United States reinstated its blockade of Iranian ships transiting the strait and demanded reimbursement equal to 20% of the value of other cargo. Tehran accepted the principle that safe passage could carry compensation but called 20% excessive. Source reporting estimated the proposed U.S. charge at roughly $32 million for a fully loaded very large crude carrier at current prices, compared with previously reported Iranian tolls reaching about $2 million. The administration had not explained enforcement, administration or coordination with Gulf allies by the source cutoff.

That uncertainty added to renewed strikes and conflicting claims about whether the strait remained open. WTI closed at 79.27 and Brent at 84.38, each up about 11%. The move embedded physical supply, shipping, freight and insurance risk, then transmitted into inflation compensation and the Fed path. The 2-basis-point rise in 10-year breakevens captured part of the energy premium; the tightening signal and the 4-basis-point rise in the 10-year real yield carried the policy response.

The fee proposal widened the channel beyond the barrel price. A charge based on cargo value raises delivered costs even before a physical disruption, while unclear administration raises delay and compliance costs for shipowners, refiners and insurers. Import-dependent economies absorb that pressure through trade balances and currencies; U.S. markets receive it through gasoline, freight and the expected persistence of headline inflation. That is why oil, DXY and Treasury real yields moved in the same tightening direction.

Equities showed a clear duration hierarchy. The S&P 500 fell 0.79%, the Nasdaq 100 lost 1.88%, and the semiconductor index fell 4.78%. The VIX rose 14.17% to 17.16. SK Hynix's U.S. trading loss and the broader semiconductor selloff placed the largest pressure on capital-intensive growth, even as TSMC sales confirmed strong underlying demand. The market reduced the value assigned to distant cash flows and increased the cost of financing their production.

DXY rose 0.31% and gold fell 2.46%. The dollar gained from tighter U.S. policy expectations and risk demand, while higher real yields outweighed gold's geopolitical support. Listed fixed-income gauges moved in the same direction: LQD lost 0.47%, HYG and JNK declined, and EMB fell 0.64%. The board did not indicate a generalized funding break, but it showed the energy and real-rate shock reaching Treasury-duration credit, lower-quality listed risk and external-dollar borrowers.

## Forward Market Significance

June CPI on July 14 is the first and largest test. Headline CPI follows a 0.5% monthly increase, with consensus at minus 0.1% and the calendar forecast at zero. Core CPI follows 0.2%, with consensus at 0.3% and the forecast at 0.2%. Firm core CPI extends belly pressure and validates the near-term tightening signal. Softer core inflation shifts more of the session's repricing into energy and breakevens, giving policy-sensitive real yields room to retrace. The composition across services and tariff-sensitive goods carries more information than the headline alone.

Warsh testifies later on July 14 and again on July 15. Markets already know that he dislikes conventional forward guidance; the unresolved issue is how he maps inflation, labor and growth into policy. A clear reaction function can reduce event premium while preserving a restrictive stance. Continued ambiguity leaves each release carrying more policy information and keeps the 2- to 5-year sector exposed to repricing. The July 13 statement gives lawmakers and investors a concrete comparison for the chairman's response.

PPI, the Beige Book and the 17-week bill auction form the July 15 sequence. PPI follows a 1.1% monthly increase, with consensus at minus 0.1% and the calendar forecast at 0.1%. Producer prices identify pipeline pressure after the oil move; the Beige Book adds regional evidence on pricing power, demand and the ability to pass through costs. The 17-week bill auction then measures cash demand around the releases. Firm pipeline prices and strong pricing power preserve the real-yield burden; soft cost pressure and weaker demand support duration. Auction demand determines how much event premium also reaches front-end liquidity.

Retail sales, initial claims, the Bank of Korea and 4- and 8-week bill auctions follow on July 16. Retail sales come after a 0.9% gain, with consensus at 0.2% and the forecast at 0.5%. Claims follow 215,000, with consensus at 218,000. Resilient demand and contained claims support a restrictive policy path; weaker consumption and rising claims cap real yields while shifting the credit discussion toward growth. The Bank of Korea enters at 2.50% with imported-energy, currency and financial-stability pressure in view. Bill demand after CPI and retail sales provides a separate reading on cash allocation.

Housing starts, industrial production and preliminary Michigan inflation expectations arrive July 17. Starts follow 1.177 million, with consensus at 1.30 million and the forecast at 1.20 million. Industrial production follows 0.1%, with consensus at 0.2% and the forecast at 0.4%. Michigan one-year expectations previously stood at 4.6% and five-year expectations at 3.3%, against forecasts of 4.3% and 3.1%. Housing and production measure the demand cost of elevated rates; household expectations measure whether the energy shock is reaching credibility.

Three- and six-month bill auctions on July 20 complete the seven-day horizon. By then the market will have CPI, two days of Warsh testimony, PPI, regional business evidence, consumption, labor and inflation expectations. Treasury maturities need core disinflation or weaker demand to reverse the July 13 belly selloff. Credit needs stable rates and orderly primary execution, including Pinewood, to preserve breadth. Global duration needs contained energy pressure and clearer local policy paths. EM needs stable dollar liquidity and successful sovereign execution. The sequence is close enough that distant second-half themes do not belong ahead of these dated tests.

section_summaries.summary: Trump's blockade and proposed 20% Hormuz cargo charge pushed oil sharply higher, while Waller tied another hot core-inflation print to possible near-term tightening. The 5Y led Treasury losses and both real yields and breakevens rose. Semiconductors, listed credit and EM debt then carried the increase in discount rates and external funding costs beyond the energy market.
section_summaries.market_read: A named energy-policy escalation and a named Fed signal tightened the same close.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Inflation compensation and the real discount rate rose together across rates and funded risk.
section_summaries.next_check: CPI composition and Warsh's reaction function.

section_summaries.section_title: UST Rates, Fed Pricing, Real Yields, And Curve Structure
section_summaries.summary: The 5Y rose 7 basis points and led a broad Treasury selloff after Waller put near-term tightening back into the policy debate. The 10Y real yield added 4 basis points and the breakeven added 2, while belly butterflies and three- to twelve-month bills also cheapened. CPI and Warsh now determine how much of the move persists in the policy horizon.
section_summaries.market_read: Waller's signal concentrated a combined real-rate and inflation adjustment in the belly.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The curve repriced policy persistence rather than only the spot cost of oil.
section_summaries.next_check: Core CPI, Warsh testimony and bill demand.

section_summaries.summary: AI end demand stayed firm, but SK Hynix's post-listing decline and the SOX selloff showed weaker tolerance for valuation and capital intensity. Hyperscaler funding is broadening from unsecured bonds toward data-center, tenant-backed and private structures as absorption limits emerge. Pinewood's proposed sterling issue provides a separate test of crossover order books, pricing and secondary performance.
section_summaries.market_read: AI financing remains available, but market absorption now demands more structure and compensation.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Strong technology demand no longer guarantees an easy financing channel for the required capital.
section_summaries.next_check: Primary books, concessions and Pinewood execution.

section_summaries.section_title: Global Duration, Central Banks, And Fiscal Transmission
section_summaries.summary: Japan denied an immediate change to GPIF allocation, weakening the home-bias support previously priced into the yen and JGBs. Europe faced the opposite constraint as economists cut 2026 growth to 0.5% while retaining a 2.8% inflation forecast after renewed Hormuz disruption. Global duration received neither a large savings-allocation shift nor clean energy-driven disinflation.
section_summaries.market_read: Japan removed an allocation catalyst while Europe absorbed imported-energy stagflation.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: Neither developed-market channel supplied a broad offset to higher U.S. real yields.
section_summaries.next_check: Yen and JGB stability, European energy pressure and the BOK decision.

section_summaries.section_title: EM Sovereigns, Dollar Liquidity, And Debt-Management Risk
section_summaries.summary: DXY rose and EMB fell, but sovereign access remained differentiated. Indonesia retained investment grade around its 3% deficit ceiling, Chile held an external issuance window near an 82-basis-point spread, and Kenya considered a $500 million buyback. Nigeria and Ethiopia maintained restrictive policy as inflation credibility and maturity management outweighed broad beta under tighter dollar and oil conditions.
section_summaries.market_read: Fiscal anchors and active debt management preserved selective EM access.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The common external shock left less room for weak policy execution or passive refinancing.
section_summaries.next_check: Local FX, Chile issuance and Kenya's new-debt terms.

section_summaries.summary: Trump reinstated the blockade of Iranian ships transiting Hormuz and demanded reimbursement equal to 20% of other cargo value, adding freight and implementation risk to renewed strikes. WTI and Brent rose about 11%, DXY and VIX gained, and gold fell as real yields increased. Technology, listed credit and EMB confirmed pressure on long-duration and external-dollar assets.
section_summaries.market_read: The Hormuz policy escalation transmitted through oil, real yields, volatility and dollar liquidity.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: The shock raised delivered energy costs and the discount rate applied to funded assets at once.
section_summaries.next_check: Hormuz implementation, oil persistence and SOX stabilization.

section_summaries.summary: CPI and Warsh testimony lead the seven-day sequence because they directly test Waller's tightening signal and the split between energy and core inflation. PPI, the Beige Book and bill auctions follow with pipeline-price, pricing-power and liquidity evidence. Retail sales, claims, the BOK, housing, production and Michigan expectations then test demand, global policy and inflation credibility.
section_summaries.market_read: Inflation composition comes first, followed by policy interpretation, demand and liquidity.
section_summaries.why_it_matters: These dated releases determine the persistence and breadth of the July 13 repricing.
section_summaries.next_check: CPI, Warsh, PPI, retail sales and claims.

methodology_note: Synthesized market analysis from market levels and reporting context. Forward Market Significance carries the next market checks.
